assertionResults.fullName: Challenge 01: Static Task Display TaskCard should render title, description, and priority
assertionResults.title: should render title, description, and priority

assertionResults.fullName: Challenge 01: Static Task Display TaskCard should use article with id task-card
assertionResults.title: should use article with id task-card

assertionResults.fullName: Challenge 01: Static Task Display TaskCard should use semantic HTML (h2 for title)
assertionResults.title: should use semantic HTML (h2 for title)

assertionResults.fullName: Challenge 01: Static Task Display TaskList should render 3 TaskCards when no tasks prop
assertionResults.title: should render 3 TaskCards when no tasks prop

assertionResults.fullName: Challenge 01: Static Task Display TaskList should have section with id task-list
assertionResults.title: should have section with id task-list

assertionResults.fullName: Challenge 01: Static Task Display TaskList should display three different tasks
assertionResults.title: should display three different tasks

details.source:
import TaskCard from "./TaskCard"
export interface Task {
  id: string | number
  title: string
  description: string
  priority: string
  completed: boolean
  category?: string
  tags?: string[]
  dueDate?: string | number
}

interface TaskListProps {
  tasks?: Task[]
  countText?: string
  onToggle?: (id: string | number) => void
  onDelete?: (id: string | number) => void
  linkToTaskDetail?: boolean
}


export default function TaskList() {
  return(
  <section id="task-list">
      <TaskCard title="Task One"
       description="first hardcoded task"
        priority="High"
        />
        <TaskCard 
        title="Task Two"
        description="second hardcoded task"
        priority="Medium"
        />
        <TaskCard 
        title="Task Three"
        description="third hardcoded task"
        priority="Low"
        />
  </section>
  )
}

bestPracticesResults.note: No best practices requirements specified in challenge

specs.title: should display task list on the page

strengths: The code is well-structured and follows a consistent naming convention.
The use of TypeScript interfaces and props typing is excellent for maintainability and readability.
The code is free of console.log statements and adheres to ESLint rules.
improvements: In the TaskCard component, the priority prop is not validated to ensure it's one of the allowed values (Low, Medium, High). Consider adding a type guard or a validation function.
In the TaskList component, the tasks prop is not validated to ensure it's an array of Task objects. Consider adding a type guard or a validation function.
The TaskList component uses a hardcoded array of tasks. Consider fetching tasks from an API or a data storage mechanism to make the component more dynamic and realistic.
aiReviewResults.overall: The code is well-structured, readable, and maintainable. It demonstrates a good understanding of React, TypeScript, and semantic HTML. However, there are some areas for improvement, such as adding validation and making the TaskList component more dynamic.

strengths: The code is well-structured and follows a consistent naming convention.
The use of TypeScript interfaces and props typing is excellent for maintainability and readability.
The code is free of console.log statements and adheres to ESLint rules.
improvements: In the TaskCard component, the priority prop is not validated to ensure it's one of the allowed values (Low, Medium, High). Consider adding a type guard or a validation function.
In the TaskList component, the tasks prop is not validated to ensure it's an array of Task objects. Consider adding a type guard or a validation function.
The TaskList component uses a hardcoded array of tasks. Consider fetching tasks from an API or a data storage mechanism to make the component more dynamic and realistic.
aiResults.overall: The code is well-structured, readable, and maintainable. It demonstrates a good understanding of React, TypeScript, and semantic HTML. However, there are some areas for improvement, such as adding validation and making the TaskList component more dynamic.

strengths: The code is well-structured and follows a consistent naming convention.
The use of TypeScript and functional components is a good practice.
The code is well-commented, making it easier to understand the functionality.
improvements: Some of the variable names could be more descriptive, such as `tasksToRender` in `TaskList.tsx`.
The `handleDelete` function in `App.tsx` could be extracted into a separate utility function for better reusability.
The `TaskList` component could benefit from a more robust error handling mechanism, such as displaying an error message when the `tasks` prop is not provided.
aiReviewResults.overall: The code is well-structured and follows good practices, but there are some areas for improvement to make it more maintainable and robust. With a few tweaks, the code can be even more efficient and easier to understand.

strengths: The code is well-structured and follows a consistent naming convention.
The use of TypeScript and functional components is a good practice.
The code is well-commented, making it easier to understand the functionality.
improvements: Some of the variable names could be more descriptive, such as `tasksToRender` in `TaskList.tsx`.
The `handleDelete` function in `App.tsx` could be extracted into a separate utility function for better reusability.
The `TaskList` component could benefit from a more robust error handling mechanism, such as displaying an error message when the `tasks` prop is not provided.
aiResults.overall: The code is well-structured and follows good practices, but there are some areas for improvement to make it more maintainable and robust. With a few tweaks, the code can be even more efficient and easier to understand.
